stem: A astronomy dataset contains 50 observations of apparent magnitude. The data is believed to come from a mixture of two populations: foreground stars (mean ≈ 83.4 mag, SD ≈ 13.7) and background galaxies (mean ≈ 196.3 mag, SD ≈ 21.0). The proportion of foreground stars in the sample is uncertain, estimated between 48% and 62%. The observed overall mean is 134.2 mag with SD = 58.8. Estimate the true population mean of apparent magnitude, accounting for the uncertainty in the mixing proportion.

ground_truth.answer: Mixture mean = w×83.4 + (1-w)×196.3. As w varies from 0.48 to 0.62, the mixture mean ranges from 126.3 to 142.1. With sampling variability, CI = [110.0, 158.4]. Point estimate at w=0.55: 134.2 mag.

solution_steps: Mixture mean formula: μ_mix = w × μ_A + (1-w) × μ_B.
At w=0.48: μ_mix = 142.1 (more of background galaxies).
At w=0.62: μ_mix = 126.3 (more of foreground stars).
Combined interval [110.0, 158.4] accounts for both mixing uncertainty and sampling variability.
common_failure_modes: Using the simple average of the two component means without weighting
Treating the mixing proportion as known instead of uncertain
Ignoring that the overall SD (58.8) is inflated by the between-component variation
